content.text: Separating
what an agent must do
from
how it must behave while doing it

is the single most impactful structural decision in agentic prompt design. When these two concerns are blurred into one block of text, multi-step agent loops become brittle: behavior drifts turn-by-turn, debugging is guesswork, and task templates cannot be reused across sub-agents. Keeping them in distinct layers — instructions in the system prompt, tasks in the user message — makes prompts testable, composable, and maintainable at production scale.
What it is
Every API call to a large language model carries a structured message list. Within that list, two conceptually different kinds of content appear:
instructions
and
tasks
. An
instruction
is a standing directive that shapes agent behavior across all turns: role definition, safety constraints, tool-use policy, output format defaults. A
task

is a bounded, ephemeral objective scoped to a single turn or loop iteration: "classify this intent", "call the lookup_order tool with order_id=X", "draft a reply from the data returned in tool result ID 3".
The distinction maps directly onto message roles. Instructions live in the
system
role (or in an Anthropic-style
developer
turn). Tasks live in each
user

turn. Because the model processes the full message list at every inference call, instructions act as a persistent context layer while tasks change with every request. Neither concept is unique to AI — a professional handbook (instructions) paired with a daily work ticket (task) captures the same split.
Anatomy comparison
Dimension
Instructions
Tasks
Lifespan
Stable for an entire session or deployment
Scoped to one model call or loop iteration
Message role
system / developer
user
Author
Developer / operator
Orchestrator / runtime
Purpose
Govern behavior, safety, policy
Define the deliverable for this step
Change trigger
Product policy update
New user request or agent step
Why it matters

A single-shot chatbot can tolerate blended prompts because there is only one call. Agent loops invoke the model dozens or hundreds of times per workflow. Without a stable instruction layer, each iteration is free to renegotiate the behavioral contract: the model may silently change its output format, skip a verification step it followed last turn, or omit a required tool call because the wording of the task implied it was optional. These failures are notoriously hard to reproduce because the model's behavior is sensitive to small phrasing differences injected by the orchestrator.

Without a well-defined task layer, the opposite problem emerges: the model optimizes for general helpfulness rather than the specific deliverable the orchestrator expects. This produces outputs that are plausible in isolation but mismatched to the next stage of the pipeline — an orchestrator expecting structured JSON receives a prose answer; a downstream tool receives arguments with unexpected field names. Most production agentic failures trace to this ambiguity rather than to model capability limits.

Separating the layers also makes prompts auditable. When a security team reviews what an agent is allowed to do, they audit the instruction layer. When an engineer debugs an incorrect output, they can ask "was the task under-specified?" or "did an instruction conflict with the task?", narrowing the search space immediately.
Benefits
Predictable multi-turn behavior
— instructions anchor the agent's persona, safety rules, and tool policies so they cannot drift between loop iterations.
Faster debugging
— a mismatch between expected and actual output is immediately classified as either an instruction failure (wrong policy) or a task failure (wrong objective), halving the search space.
Reusable instruction sets
— a single system prompt can be paired with dozens of different task templates across feature surfaces without duplicating policy text.
Composable task templates
— parameterized task strings can be generated dynamically by the orchestrator from user input, retrieved context, and tool results, without touching the stable instruction layer.
Better tool routing
— tasks can nominate specific tools or exclude others for this step; instructions define global tool permissions, preventing task-level overrides from bypassing policy.
Cleaner version control
— instruction and task layers have independent change cadences, so git history reflects meaningful policy vs. capability changes separately.
Safer agent handoffs
— when one sub-agent delegates to another, it can pass a task without also needing to repeat the behavioral contract, reducing context consumption.
Trade-offs
Context window cost

— a verbose instruction layer occupies tokens on every call. At 200 turns with a 2,000-token system prompt, the instruction layer alone consumes 400 K tokens in a session. Prompt caching mitigates this but adds engineering overhead.
Conflict risk

— if a task specifies a behavior that contradicts an instruction ("return plain text" in the task vs. "always respond in JSON" in the system prompt), the model's resolution depends on the provider, model version, and token position — not on developer intent.
Over-decomposition tax
— splitting a workflow into many micro-tasks (one API call per trivial step) adds latency, cost, and orchestration complexity with no reliability gain when the steps are tightly coupled.
Instruction staleness

— a stable instruction layer is only an advantage if the instructions actually stay current. Outdated constraints that were never updated become silent anti-features that confuse the model and the team.
Reduced flexibility for creative tasks
— highly prescriptive task templates reduce the model's ability to adapt to edge cases not anticipated during design.
How it works

The model receives the full message list as input. Every block in that list is concatenated (with role markers) into the effective prompt before tokenization. The model therefore sees instructions and tasks in the same context window, but their separation matters for two reasons: position effects and the author contract.

Position effects are real: content near the beginning of the context (system prompt) and near the end (the latest user turn) receives disproportionate attention from the model due to how transformers weight context. Instructions placed in the system prompt sit at the start, giving them strong anchoring. The current task, as the last user message, sits at the end — the model's "recency focus" zone. This positional split naturally reinforces the logical separation: policy at the start, action at the end.

The author contract is the human dimension: the instruction layer is authored once by a developer or product team and reviewed for safety. The task layer is generated at runtime by an orchestrator, often from untrusted user input. This means the instruction layer must be defensive (it should not trust or echo back content from the task layer), and the task layer must be validated before injection (inputs sanitized, schema-checked, length-capped).
The rendering stack

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  API Call Structure                                    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  role: "system"                                        │
│    [Instructions: persona, rules, tool policy]        │
│                                                        │
│  role: "user"   (turn N-1 — previous task response)   │
│    [Assistant's last output or tool result]           │
│                                                        │
│  role: "user"   (turn N — current task)               │
│    [Task: objective + input data + constraints]        │
│                                                        │
│  ──► Model generates response or tool call             │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Task anatomy — four required components
Objective
— one imperative sentence stating the deliverable: "Extract all dates in ISO-8601 format from the passage below."
Inputs
— the data the model may use, clearly delimited. The model must not invent data absent from this section.
Constraints
— turn-level limits: output length cap, language restriction, allowed tool set for this step, prohibited actions.
Completion signal
— how the orchestrator detects task completion: a specific JSON key, a DONE token, or a structured stop sequence.

Completion signals are often omitted by beginners and are the leading cause of orchestrator hangs. Without an explicit signal, the orchestrator must parse free-form text to decide whether the step is finished — a fragile heuristic that fails on longer or multilingual outputs.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the structural relationship between the instruction layer, the task layer, the LLM, and the orchestrator in a standard agentic loop. Instructions flow once per session; tasks are injected on every iteration.

attrs.alt: Agent prompt architecture showing instructions (stable, system prompt) and tasks (ephemeral, user message) feeding into the LLM context window, controlled by an orchestrator loop

content.text: Key structural observations: (1) the instruction layer and task layer enter the context window through different roles, giving the model a consistent signal about which content is policy and which is objective; (2) the orchestrator is the only component that touches the task layer at runtime — it neither reads nor modifies the instruction layer; (3) the loop arrow represents the iterative call pattern: the orchestrator consumes the model's response, decides whether the workflow continues, and if so constructs the next task.
When to use it

The instruction/task separation is mandatory for any agent that makes more than one model call, calls tools, or delegates to sub-agents. It is optional but still beneficial for single-shot structured-output calls, because explicit task framing reduces format errors even without multi-turn concerns.
Use it when
The agent runs a loop of two or more model calls to complete a workflow.
The agent uses tools (function calling) where tool-use policy must be globally enforced.
Multiple orchestrators or sub-agents share the same behavioral policy.
The instruction layer is owned by a different team (security, compliance) than the task templates.
Task inputs contain untrusted or user-supplied content that must not alter behavioral policy.
Avoid strict separation when
A single short prompt is sufficient and will never be iterated into a loop.
The model is used for pure retrieval augmentation with no behavioral policy at all.

The task and the behavioral rule are genuinely inseparable (e.g., "translate this sentence and always capitalize proper nouns" where the capitalization rule is meaningful only for this specific document).
How to implement
Implementing the split requires two authoring artifacts — an instruction document and a set of task templates — and one runtime component: the orchestrator that renders templates at loop time.

Draft the system prompt with only standing instructions. Omit any reference to specific user input, current data, or session-level goals. Test it alone against a diverse range of synthetic tasks to confirm it does not inadvertently restrict valid behaviors.

Create parameterized task templates — strings with named placeholders such as {input_text}, {order_id}, or {tool_result}. Store them in source control alongside the system prompt, versioned independently.
Validate task inputs before rendering. Apply length caps, encoding checks, and schema validation to all external data before it is inserted into a template.
Inject retrieved context and tool results inside the task block using clear XML-style delimiters (<context>...</context>, <tool_result>...</tool_result>). Do not paste context into the system prompt.
Define completion signals in every task template. Prefer structured signals (a JSON key or a fixed stop token) over prose detection.

Log the rendered task string separately from the system prompt when debugging. Most logging frameworks treat the full message list as a blob; manually recording each layer separately saves hours in production postmortems.
Version instructions and task templates independently in git. Use semantic versioning and tag the system prompt version in metadata alongside every logged session so regressions can be bisected.
A minimal Python implementation using the Anthropic SDK follows:

import anthropic

SYSTEM_INSTRUCTIONS = """
You are a production incident analyst.
Rules:
- Use only evidence present in the provided input; never invent data.
- If information is insufficient, respond with {"status": "insufficient_data"}.
- Always respond in valid JSON matching the schema given in the task.
- Do not call any tool unless the task explicitly names it.
"""

TASK_TEMPLATE = """
Task: Identify the root-cause error in the log excerpt below.
Input:
<logs>
{log_text}
</logs>
Output schema:
{{"error_line": <int>, "message": <str>, "confidence": "high"|"medium"|"low"}}
"""

def analyze_logs(log_text: str) -> dict:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    task = TASK_TEMPLATE.format(log_text=log_text)
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=SYSTEM_INSTRUCTIONS,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": task}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

if __name__ == "__main__":
    sample_logs = """2025-01-10 14:32:01 INFO  Starting job
2025-01-10 14:32:04 ERROR NullPointerException at OrderService.java:142
2025-01-10 14:32:04 ERROR   at checkout.process(checkout.java:88)"""
    print(analyze_logs(sample_logs))
    # Expected: {"error_line": 2, "message": "NullPointerException at OrderService.java:142", "confidence": "high"}
Examples
Example 1 — Weak: merged instruction and task

The following merged prompt is typical of early-stage prototypes. It conflates policy ("never make up data"), persona ("helpful assistant"), and objective ("summarize the contract") into one paragraph.

# Anti-pattern: instruction and task merged
merged_prompt = """
You are a helpful assistant. Never make up data. The user has provided
a contract below. Summarize it in three bullet points. Be concise.

CONTRACT:
{contract_text}
"""

# Problems:
# 1. "Never make up data" repeats on every call — wastes tokens at scale.
# 2. If you need a different task (translate, extract clauses), you must
#    rewrite the entire prompt, including the policy text.
# 3. Debugging: if the summary is wrong, you cannot isolate whether the
#    policy or the objective caused the failure.
Example 2 — Strong: separated layers
The same workflow rewritten with clean separation. The system prompt holds all policy; the task template holds all objective logic.

import anthropic

# ── Layer 1: Instructions (system prompt, stable) ──────────────────
SYSTEM = """
You are a legal-document analyst.
Policy:
- Base every response strictly on the provided document text.
- If a requested item is absent from the document, state \"Not found\".
- Never add legal interpretation beyond what the text explicitly states.
Output format: always match the schema specified in the task.
"""

# ── Layer 2: Task templates (runtime, ephemeral) ────────────────────
SUMMARIZE_TASK = """
Task: Summarize the contract in exactly three bullet points.
Each bullet: one sentence, ≤ 20 words.
Output format: JSON array of strings.
Completion signal: respond with the JSON array and nothing else.

<document>
{document_text}
</document>
"""

EXTRACT_PARTIES_TASK = """
Task: Extract all named parties and their roles from the contract.
Output format: JSON array of {{"name": str, "role": str}}.
Completion signal: respond with the JSON array and nothing else.

<document>
{document_text}
</document>
"""

def run_task(task_template: str, document_text: str) -> str:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    task = task_template.format(document_text=document_text)
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": task}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text

# The same system prompt handles both tasks without modification.
# Swapping the task template is the only change needed per capability.
Example 3 — Agent loop with dynamic task injection
Real-world example

A B2B customer-support platform processes 50,000 tickets per day through an agentic pipeline. Each ticket triggers a 4-to-8 step loop: intent classification, order lookup via tool, policy check, draft generation, tone review, and optional escalation decision. The system prompt (instructions) encodes 14 behavioral rules including: escalate billing disputes to a human within two turns; never promise refunds; call
lookup_order
before answering any shipping question; respond in the customer's detected language.

Each loop step receives a narrowly scoped task. Step 1: "Classify the customer intent from the message below as one of: SHIPPING_INQUIRY | BILLING_DISPUTE | PRODUCT_QUESTION | OTHER. Output: JSON {\"intent\": string}. DONE when json is emitted." Step 3: "Given order data from tool result below, determine whether the delivery is within the stated SLA. Output: JSON {\"within_sla\": bool, \"days_late\": int|null}." The tasks are short, parameterized strings rendered by the orchestrator.

When a regulatory change required the platform to add a new escalation rule (escalate returns over $500 to a senior agent), the engineering team edited one line in the system prompt and ran their instruction-layer test suite. No task template changed. The regression suite (100 synthetic tickets per intent class) confirmed behavior in 12 minutes, and the change shipped same day. Had the rule been embedded in individual task strings, the team would have needed to locate and update every task that touched returns — a risk-prone search across dozens of templates.
Common mistakes
Putting user input in the system prompt

# WRONG: user input in system prompt pollutes all future turns
def bad_agent(user_query):
    system = f"""
    You are a helpful assistant. The user asked: {user_query}
    Answer helpfully and concisely.
    """
    # Problem: user_query in system prompt is cached and can persist
    # across sessions depending on provider implementation. Untrusted
    # content in the system prompt is also a prompt injection vector.

# CORRECT: user input goes in the user message (task layer)
SYSTEM = "You are a helpful assistant. Answer concisely."

def good_agent(user_query):
    task = f"Question: {user_query}"
    # The system prompt is now clean and cacheable.
Vague tasks without completion signals

# WRONG: no deliverable, no completion signal
"Help the user with their order issue."

# CORRECT: specific deliverable + completion signal
"Task: Determine whether order #38291 is eligible for a free reship.
Use only data from the <order_details> block below.
Output: JSON {"eligible": bool, "reason": string}.
Completion signal: emit the JSON object on a single line with no prose.

<order_details>
{order_details_json}
</order_details>"
Restating instructions in every task
— "Never make up data. Be concise. You are a helpful assistant." repeated in 20 task templates wastes tokens and introduces divergence risk when the real instruction is later updated.
Conflicting directives
— system prompt says "respond in JSON" but a task says "explain in plain English". The model resolves conflicts non-deterministically.
Over-decomposing trivial steps
— splitting a three-sentence operation into three separate LLM calls adds 3× latency and cost with no reliability gain.
Skipping input validation
— injecting raw user text directly into a task template without length-capping or encoding checks is the most common prompt injection path in agentic applications.
Best practices
Use imperative verbs in task objectives: "Extract", "Compare", "Call", "Classify", "Return". Avoid hedged formulations like "try to" or "help with".
Keep instruction layers concise but complete. Aim for under 800 tokens. Every token in the system prompt pays a cost on every call.
Store instructions and task templates in source control as separate files. Treat the system prompt like a configuration artifact with its own release notes.
Use XML-style delimiters (<context>, <input>, <tool_result>) to isolate injected data from the task objective text. This reduces model confusion about which content is instruction vs. data.
Write negative constraints in instructions ("never promise refunds") and positive actions in tasks ("draft a reply using the data below"). Mixing them degrades clarity in both layers.
Run instruction-layer tests after every system prompt change. A suite of 20–50 synthetic tasks covering edge cases detects regressions before deployment.
Document the intended scope of each task template — which tools it may call, what schema it must return — in a comment block adjacent to the template.
Log the rendered task and system prompt version as separate fields in observability systems (Langfuse, Phoenix, Braintrust). This makes incident attribution to instruction vs. task changes tractable.
Performance & scalability

The instruction/task split has direct performance implications in production agent systems. The primary leverage point is prompt caching. Because the instruction layer is identical across all calls in a session (and often across sessions for the same deployment), it is an ideal candidate for provider-level prompt caching — Anthropic's cache breakpoints, for example, allow the system prompt to be cached once and reused across requests, reducing per-call latency by 30–50% and cost by up to 90% for the cached portion.

Token budget management becomes simpler with the split. The instruction layer has a known, fixed token count (measured once at deploy time). The orchestrator can compute the remaining budget available to the task, history, and response, and dynamically cap task input length to stay within the context window. Without the split, token budgeting requires parsing a merged prompt to estimate which portion is policy and which is data.
Latency profile

# Rough latency breakdown for a 10-step agent loop
# with 1,500-token system prompt + 300-token avg task

WITHOUT_CACHING = {
    'tokens_per_call': 1500 + 300,   # instructions + task
    'calls': 10,
    'total_input_tokens': 18_000,
    'prefill_latency_ms': 18_000 * 0.01,  # ~0.01ms per input token
}

WITH_INSTRUCTION_CACHING = {
    'cached_tokens_per_call': 1500,   # instructions cached after first call
    'uncached_tokens_per_call': 300,  # task tokens
    'calls': 10,
    'total_uncached_input_tokens': 300 * 10,   # 3,000
    # Effective prefill latency drops ~83% for cached portion
    # Cached reads are typically 5-10x faster than uncached
}

# The clean instruction/task split makes the caching boundary unambiguous.
# A mixed prompt cannot be split cleanly at the cache boundary.

Scalability note: as agent workflows expand to hundreds of steps or parallel sub-agents, the instruction layer becomes a shared resource. A single system prompt can be referenced by multiple parallel sub-agents running on the same deployment, all benefiting from the same cache entry. This is only possible when instructions are cleanly separated from tasks.
Tooling & ecosystem
Several frameworks and platforms have built first-class support for the instruction/task distinction, offering templating, versioning, and evaluation tooling that maps directly onto the two layers.
LangChain / LangGraph

from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate, SystemMessagePromptTemplate
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic

# Instructions live in SystemMessagePromptTemplate — stable
system_tpl = SystemMessagePromptTemplate.from_template(
    "You are a code review assistant. "
    "Only flag bugs that cause incorrect behavior. "
    "Never suggest stylistic changes unless asked."
)

# Task template — ephemeral, parameterized
task_tpl = ("{role}", "Review the following diff and list any bugs.\n\n<diff>\n{diff_text}\n</diff>")

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([system_tpl, task_tpl])
chain = prompt | ChatAnthropic(model="claude-opus-4-5")

result = chain.invoke({"role": "user", "diff_text": "- return x + 1\n+ return x - 1"})
print(result.content)
Prompt management platforms
Langfuse
— stores instructions and task templates as separate prompt versions with semantic versioning. Logs both layers per trace, enabling per-layer A/B testing.
Braintrust
— provides an eval harness where the system prompt and task templates are versioned independently and each can be evaluated against a shared test suite.
PromptLayer
— tracks system prompt and user message separately per API call, allowing cost attribution to each layer.
Weights & Biases Prompts (Weave)
— traces each layer's contribution to final outputs and enables side-by-side comparison when either layer changes.
Provider-native features

import anthropic

# Anthropic prompt caching — mark the instruction layer as cacheable
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SYSTEM = [
    {
        "type": "text",
        "text": """You are a data extraction specialist.
Rules:
- Extract only fields explicitly listed in the task schema.
- For missing fields, output null, never infer or guess.
- Respond in valid JSON matching the schema.""",
        "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},  # Cache this block
    }
]

TASK = """
Task: Extract invoice fields from the document below.
Schema: {{"invoice_number": str, "total": float, "vendor": str, "date": str}}

<document>
{document}
</document>
"""

def extract(document_text: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": TASK.format(document=document_text)}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text
Comparison with alternatives
Several alternative prompt structures exist. The table compares them against the instruction/task split across dimensions relevant to production agentic systems.
Approach
Maintainability
Debug clarity
Multi-turn stability
Token efficiency
Best for
Instructions + Tasks (separated)
High — each layer versioned independently
High — failures isolate to one layer
High — instructions stable across turns
High — instructions cacheable
All multi-step agents and tool-using workflows
Merged single prompt
Low — any change risks side effects across all behaviors
Low — cannot isolate policy vs. task failures
Low — behavior drifts as merged prompt changes
Medium — no caching benefit
Simple one-shot calls with no reuse requirement
Few-shot examples only (no explicit instructions)
Medium — examples encode behavior implicitly
Low — behavior hard to audit from examples alone
Medium — examples stay fixed but policy is implicit
Low — examples consume many tokens
Tasks where desired behavior is easier to show than describe
Chain-of-thought scratchpad (ReAct style)
Medium — prompt is structured but blends steps
Medium — reasoning trace aids but does not replace separation
Medium — behavior depends on scratchpad continuity
Low — long reasoning traces add token cost
Tasks requiring visible reasoning over ambiguous inputs
Tool schemas only (no system prompt)
Low — all policy must be in tool descriptions
Low — policy lives in JSON, hard to audit
Low — no persistent behavioral anchor
Medium
Simple tool-call shims with no behavioral policy
Security & reliability

The instruction/task boundary is the primary defense line against prompt injection in agentic systems. Prompt injection occurs when adversarial content in the task layer (typically user-supplied input or data fetched from an external source) attempts to override or hijack the instruction layer. A clean, robust instruction layer makes injection harder but not impossible — defensive prompt design is still required.
Injection hardening

import html
import re

MAX_INPUT_CHARS = 8_000

def sanitize_for_task(user_input: str) -> str:
    """Basic hardening before injecting user text into a task template."""
    # 1. Length cap — prevents context window stuffing attacks
    text = user_input[:MAX_INPUT_CHARS]

    # 2. Escape XML-special characters so injected delimiters cannot
    #    break the <input>...</input> wrapper in the task template
    text = html.escape(text)

    # 3. Strip common injection patterns (model-specific; adjust per provider)
    injection_patterns = [
        r'(?i)ignore (previous|all) instructions',
        r'(?i)you are now',
        r'(?i)system:',
        r'(?i)\[INST\]',
    ]
    for pat in injection_patterns:
        text = re.sub(pat, '[REDACTED]', text)

    return text

def build_task(user_input: str, schema: str) -> str:
    safe_input = sanitize_for_task(user_input)
    return (
        f"Task: Extract structured data from the input below.\n"
        f"Output schema: {schema}\n\n"
        f"<input>\n{safe_input}\n</input>\n"
        f"Completion signal: emit only the JSON object."
    )
Reliability patterns
Immutable instruction layer

— treat the system prompt as a read-only artifact at runtime. No orchestrator code path should construct or modify the system prompt from runtime data. Only a controlled deploy pipeline may change it.
Completion signal validation

— parse and validate the model's output against the expected schema before the orchestrator proceeds. A model that returns prose when JSON was expected should trigger a retry or escalation, not silent propagation of invalid data.
Retry budget per task
— define a maximum retry count (typically 2–3) per task. On exhaustion, the orchestrator should escalate to a human or fallback path rather than looping indefinitely.
Sensitive data handling
— PII or credentials that appear in tool results must not be echoed into subsequent task templates unless necessary, and must be redacted from all logged task strings.
Instruction audit trail

— log the semantic version of the system prompt with every production session. When a customer reports unexpected behavior, the instruction layer active during that session can be retrieved and reproduced exactly.
Mental model
Think of
instructions as the employee handbook
and
tasks as the ticket assigned today

. The handbook covers how the organization operates: escalation paths, communication standards, what is out of scope, and what tools the employee may use. It does not change when a new ticket arrives. The ticket describes exactly what to do today: which account to handle, what data to process, and what constitutes completion.

A new employee who only reads the handbook cannot start work — they have no ticket. An employee who only receives a ticket without a handbook cannot function reliably — they do not know the rules. Both layers are required, and neither substitutes for the other. The distinction also maps to reliability: when a customer complaint arrives, the manager asks "did the employee follow the handbook?" (instruction failure) or "was the ticket unclear?" (task failure) — the same diagnostic split applies when debugging agent outputs.
Key takeaways
Instructions define persistent behavioral policy (role, safety, tool permissions) and belong in the system prompt, stable across all loop iterations.
Tasks define the ephemeral, bounded objective for a single turn — they carry the deliverable, input data, turn-level constraints, and a completion signal.
Every well-formed task includes four components: objective, inputs, constraints, and a completion signal; omitting any one degrades reliability.
The instruction/task split directly enables prompt caching, reducing latency and cost by up to 90% for the cached instruction portion.
Instruction-layer changes (policy updates) and task-layer changes (new capabilities) have independent change cadences; versioning them separately prevents accidental regressions in either direction.
The instruction layer is the primary defense against prompt injection; user-supplied content must never reach the system prompt at runtime.
Completion signals are not optional in agentic loops; without them the orchestrator cannot reliably detect partial work or trigger retries.
When an agent output is wrong, the instruction/task split allows immediate classification of the failure as a policy error or a task-specification error, cutting debug time significantly.
